briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. Welcome to the climate briefing for June 2, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Intensifying Climate Crisis: Immediate Impacts Domestically and Internationally
sections.content: Recently, immediate and severe impacts of climate change have been widely detected both domestically and internationally. In Korea, extreme climate change threatens the production of potatoes and sweet potatoes, including 'Summi potatoes' [1, 8, 10, 103]. Early heatwaves and record-high temperatures have also led to an emergency in the supply and demand of agricultural and livestock products [2, 17, 27, 29]. Alongside warnings of intensifying wildfire damage and an impending water scarcity crisis [4, 6, 120], the World Meteorological Organization has projected an 80% chance of El Niño occurring this summer, urging preparedness for extreme weather events such as heatwaves and floods [104, 131]. Already, 30 million climate refugees have been displaced [22], and complex global crises are deepening, including the warming and acidification of Biscayne Bay and an increase in uninsurable areas within Europe [109, 111, 125].

sections.title: 🌱 Climate Change Adaptation and Carbon Neutrality Transition Efforts
sections.content: Despite the intensifying climate crisis, various regions and organizations continue their efforts in climate change adaptation and the transition to carbon neutrality. Domestically, Sokcho City has established comprehensive measures for heatwave response [3, 30], and Seoul City is actively pursuing adaptation policies, such as developing climate-resilient vegetable varieties strong against heatwaves [16, 24, 28]. In the agricultural sector, Jeongeup City has begun transplanting 'Dalhami,' a new rice variety developed to cope with climate change [11, 12, 15, 23, 26]. The Rural Development Administration is accelerating the commercialization of low-methane feed ingredients to achieve carbon neutrality in the livestock sector [50]. Furthermore, local governments' carbon neutrality efforts are showing tangible results, with Yongin Special City achieving 95% of its carbon neutrality target and reducing greenhouse gas emissions by 320,000 tons [25, 34, 35, 40, 54, 56, 57, 59, 61, 62, 65, 70]. Industrial efforts are also continuing, including Lotte Innovate's participation in data center carbon neutrality R&D [33, 39, 45, 51, 67] and the push for introducing SAF production tax credits [37]. Citizen participation and the role of religious communities are also being emphasized, as seen in Yeongcheon City's citizen education on climate change response [5, 14, 20, 21] and the Jogye Order's proposal for Buddhist action for 'Carbon Neutrality and Life Transformation' [43, 60, 68].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and Current Challenges
sections.content: The transition to renewable energy has become a core driver in responding to the climate crisis, actively pursued both domestically and internationally. Korea and China are addressing the climate crisis through clean energy technology cooperation [7, 13, 49, 75], and the Korea Environment Corporation has declared RE100, committing to convert 100% of its electricity consumption to renewable energy by 2030 [80, 93, 96]. Companies are expanding their use of renewable energy; Samsung Electronics signed its first Direct Power Purchase Agreement (DPPA) in Vietnam to supply renewable energy to its smartphone factory [79, 85], and Digital Realty's AI data center achieved a 93% renewable energy application rate [87, 97]. However, this transition process is fraught with challenges. As the proportion of renewable energy generation increases, a legal vacuum for output curtailment has emerged [78], and issues like 'grid lock,' such as warnings of a 'halving' in new solar installation volumes, are being raised [88], making grid stability an urgent concern. Furthermore, while many pledges to attract data centers were made in local elections, only 5% of candidates presented renewable energy measures [90], leading to calls for policy improvements and a balanced energy transition strategy through the active utilization of clean fuels [76].

sections.title: 🌍 International Climate Response and New Trends
sections.content: The climate crisis is generating new discussions and trends at various levels within the international community. The U.S. decision to dismantle its ocean observation network raises concerns that it could pose a significant threat to the collection of essential climate crisis data [19], potentially weakening a crucial foundation for climate science research and prediction. Analysis suggesting that 'staying cool' is becoming a new indicator of inequality amidst climate change [122] points to the climate crisis as an issue that exacerbates socio-economic imbalances. Furthermore, there is growing interest in the widespread impacts on ecosystems and daily life, such as large lakes in northern Canada showing signs of biological reset due to climate change [123], and the effects of climate change on coffee production [119]. Alongside these international discussions, various nations are making multifaceted efforts to respond to the climate crisis, including the Philippines seeking support to address climate change threats [130].

briefing.closing: As we have observed in today's briefing, the climate crisis is intensifying on a global scale. However, nations and local communities continue their efforts in adaptation and mitigation. Through sustained attention and cooperation, we must overcome the climate crisis and build a sustainable future. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.original_title: Prepare for imminent return of El Niño, UN warns
articles.description: UN agency predicts phenomenon that supercharges weather extremes has 80% chance of forming before SeptemberThe world must prepare for the imminent return of El Niño and the supercharged weather extremes it brings, the UN has warned.The powerful natural weather pattern, which raises global temperatures and worsens some rainfall, has an 80% chance of forming before September and a 90% chance of persisting until November, the World Meteorological Organization (WMO) said on Tuesday. Continue reading

summary.phenomenon: The World Meteorological Organization (WMO) announced on Tuesday that there is an 80% chance of the El Niño phenomenon forming before September and a 90% chance of it persisting until November, warning of its imminent return.
summary.cause: El Niño is a powerful natural weather pattern that raises global temperatures and exacerbates rainfall in some regions.
summary.outlook: As El Niño amplifies extreme weather events, the world must prepare for its imminent return and the extreme weather phenomena it will bring.
